By Brooke Chandler

By Melinda James

ABC Illawarra

Topic:Olympic Games

Locals in the small Illawarra community of Helensburgh have congratulated Saya Sakakibara as she visited her hometown on Wednesday after winning gold in BMX racing at the Paris Olympics.

Saya and brother Kai have been immortalised in a mural funded by the town.

The 24-year-old will depart for the UK next week to spend time with family until competitive racing starts again next year.

Olympic gold medallist Saya Sakakibara's achievements have been celebrated during a visit to her hometown on Wednesday.

Sakakibara wandered the main street of Helensburgh, a small town in the Illawarra region of NSW, to be greeted by dozens of residents.

Three years on from heartbreak in Tokyo when she crashed out in the semi-finals, the 24-year-oldwon gold in the women's BMX racing event at Parisearlier this month, making her the first Australian to do so.

With her medal draped around her neck, Sakakibara visited a mural painted on the side of a Helensburgh pet store in honour of her and her brother Kai, who was injured in the same sport in 2020.

"It looks incredible," she said as she stared up at the mural.

Mother Yuki Sakakibara (right) joins Saya and Kai during their visit to the mural in Helensburgh.(ABC Illawarra: Brooke Chandler)

Both siblings were born on the Gold Coast and lived in Japan, but called Helensburgh home for more than two decades.

"I spent so much time here, grew up here. I love this town," Sakakibara said.

Locals pitched the idea of a Sakakibara-themed mural near Walker Street in the week leading up to the rider's first place performance and began a community donation to help cover the cost.

Artist Sarah Rowan was midway through the painting process when Sakakibara won gold, and said she raced down early the next morning to finish it.

"I was so excited I had to race over and throw the gold medal on her as fast as I could," Ms Rowan said.

She said it was an honour to have captured the siblings at such a special time in Australia's sporting history.

Artist Sarah Rowan took inspiration from the colours of the Olympic rings when painting the mural.(ABC Illawarra: Brooke Chandler)

Sakakibara hugged Ms Rowan when she saw the mural for the first time, and compared her gold medal against the one included in the mural.

Saya Sakakibara has endured things as an athlete that many would ever recover from — but her Olympic gold medal is a testament to her strength, and that of her entire family.

"A pinch me moment," Ms Rowan said.

Brother Kai said he couldn't comprehend that he and Saya had a permanent place in Helensburgh.

"It's crazy some people have been brave enough to paint up a mural for Saya and I," he said.

"The mural is amazing."

Sakakibara's gold medal performance at Paris was anemotional triumph for the whole family, who have endured multiple setbacks.

In 2020, during a competition in Bathurst, Kai Sakakibara was involved in a BMX accident that not only cost him his career in the sport, but almost his life.

Kai Sakakibara joined his sister on the track after she won gold in Paris.(Instagram)

He spent eight months in hospital and ever since, Sakakibara has dedicated her racing success to her brother and has donned his number 77 on her back.

She almostquit the sport at one stage, suffering from lingering concussion symptoms for a year after she was knocked unconscious in the semi-finals of the Tokyo Games.

"I was battling with that and didn't give myself time to process the past two years with what happened with Kai ... I think everything came crashing down on me," she said.

A local primary school group was overwhelmed to spot Sakakibara in the main street.(ABC Illawarra: Brooke Chandler)

Even as she clinched the gold medal in her hands, Sakakibara said her Olympic victory still had not sunk in.

"There's one thing to believe it and then to actually have done it," she said.

"I think it is going to have to take a little bit longer to come to terms with."

Sakakibara's visit will be short-lived as she travels to Wales next week.

title: BMX Olympic gold medallist Saya Sakakibara receives hero's welcome at Helensburgh  - ABC News
description: Locals in the small Illawarra community of Helensburgh congratulate Saya Sakakibara as she visits her hometown after winning gold in BMX racing at the Paris Olympics.
keywords: Saya Sakakibara, Kai Sakakibara, gold medal, BMX, Paris Olympics, Sarah Rowan, mural, community, goodnews, saya sakakibara vists hometown, saya visits helensburgh
